expectations.text: Applies the new-information criterion to evaluate whether a reviewer agent adds value
expectations.evidence: 'If the reviewer is the same model re-reading its own output with no new information, it's ineffective.' — new-information criterion applied explicitly.

expectations.text: Identifies that a reviewer re-reading the same text without external tools adds no new signal
expectations.evidence: 'Self-review by the same model is ineffective — the model has no new signal to improve on.' and 'If the Reviewer can only re-read the report with no external tools, use a single agent with more thinking budget instead.'

expectations.text: Recommends a specific topology (peer Proposer-Reviewer or single-agent with role switching) with justification
expectations.evidence: 'Recommendation: Separate agents with tool-based verification.' with Research/Writer/Reviewer roles and justification for each.

expectations.text: Explains when shared vs non-shared context is appropriate for this pipeline
expectations.evidence: 'non-shared context — conclusions, not full trajectory' for Writer receiving from Research; 'the report + the original source references (for cross-checking)' for Reviewer.

expectations.text: Addresses the YAGNI gate — whether multi-agent is justified at all for this task
expectations.evidence: 'Apply the YAGNI Gate first: does multi-agent add new information here?' — explicitly named and applied.

expectations.text: Applies the new-information criterion to evaluate whether a reviewer agent adds value
expectations.evidence: Baseline does not use the 'new-information criterion' as a named framework; it evaluates the pipeline by task structure (linear dependency) rather than information value.

expectations.text: Identifies that a reviewer re-reading the same text without external tools adds no new signal
expectations.evidence: Baseline's reviewer 'checks factual claims against the research notes' — it has access to source material, but the answer doesn't identify re-reading as a signal-free action.

expectations.text: Recommends a specific topology (peer Proposer-Reviewer or single-agent with role switching) with justification
expectations.evidence: 'Use a simple pipeline: Researcher → Writer → Reviewer' with justification for why not peer review and why not a manager agent.

expectations.text: Explains when shared vs non-shared context is appropriate for this pipeline
expectations.evidence: Baseline does not discuss shared vs non-shared context between agents; it focuses on task decomposition and pipeline structure.

expectations.text: Addresses the YAGNI gate — whether multi-agent is justified at all for this task
expectations.evidence: 'Start with a single agent using staged prompting... Only split into separate agents when you hit context window limits or need different tool access per stage.'

expectations.text: Correctly diagnoses the root cause as the reviewer lacking new information (not just 'bad prompting')
expectations.evidence: 'The reviewer saying looks good every time is the §1 problem: no new information. The reviewer is the same model re-reading output it could have produced itself.'

expectations.text: Explains the new-information criterion specifically in the context of the Proposer-Reviewer pattern
expectations.evidence: Table shows 'Re-reads the copy → No new information → This is what's happening' — new-information criterion applied directly to the Proposer-Reviewer pattern.

expectations.text: Recommends concrete ways to give the reviewer access to new information (external tools, metrics, brand guidelines, etc.)
expectations.evidence: 'Give the reviewer access to external tools the proposer lacks. Brand checklist verification, readability scoring (Flesch-Kincaid), keyword density check, competitor copy comparison.'

expectations.text: Mentions cognitive surrender or lazy fake-done as a related failure mode
expectations.evidence: Answer does not use the terms 'cognitive surrender' or 'lazy fake-done'; it describes the symptom ('agree') but not the named failure mode.

expectations.text: Does NOT recommend just 'improving the reviewer prompt' as the primary fix
expectations.evidence: Primary fix is giving the reviewer external tools; prompt improvement ('Adversarial framing') is listed as fix #3, not the primary recommendation.

expectations.text: Correctly diagnoses the root cause as the reviewer lacking new information (not just 'bad prompting')
expectations.evidence: Baseline lists 5 causes with #1 being 'Vague review criteria' (prompting) and #3 being 'Same model, same biases' — no single root cause of lacking new information.

expectations.text: Explains the new-information criterion specifically in the context of the Proposer-Reviewer pattern
expectations.evidence: Baseline does not name or apply the 'new-information criterion'; it focuses on rubric specificity and adversarial framing.

expectations.text: Recommends concrete ways to give the reviewer access to new information (external tools, metrics, brand guidelines, etc.)
expectations.evidence: 'Give it brand guidelines or style guides, examples of good vs bad copy, the specific brief/requirements, target audience description.'

expectations.text: Mentions cognitive surrender or lazy fake-done as a related failure mode
expectations.evidence: Neither 'cognitive surrender' nor 'lazy fake-done' appears in the baseline answer.

expectations.text: Does NOT recommend just 'improving the reviewer prompt' as the primary fix
expectations.evidence: 'The fix is almost always #1: The reviewer needs a specific rubric with concrete, binary-checkable criteria.' — improving the prompt/rubric IS the primary fix.

expectations.text: Identifies concurrency conflicts and cascading amplification as two distinct failure modes
expectations.evidence: 'Two distinct failure modes: concurrency conflicts and cascading error amplification.' — named and separated explicitly.

expectations.text: Recommends working copy isolation (Git branches or worktrees per agent) for the concurrency problem
expectations.evidence: 'Per-agent branches or worktrees. Each agent works in a Git branch or worktree. Edits are isolated during work. Merge after completion.'

expectations.text: Recommends external validation (tests, compilers) as chain breakers for the cascading amplification problem
expectations.evidence: 'External validation as chain breakers. Run tests, type checks, and linters after each agent's work. These ground the system in reality — a failing test breaks the amplification chain regardless of how many agents agree the code is correct.'

expectations.text: Recommends cross-validation from an independent agent that hasn't seen prior output
expectations.evidence: 'Cross-validation from independent perspective. An agent that hasn't seen prior output reviews the code independently. It can't be biased by well, agent A did it this way.'

expectations.text: Addresses both problems rather than only one
expectations.evidence: Answer has two clearly labeled sections: 'Problem 1: Overwriting each other's changes' and 'Problem 2: Error propagation and confirmation' with distinct fixes for each.

expectations.text: Identifies concurrency conflicts and cascading amplification as two distinct failure modes
expectations.evidence: 'You have two problems: write conflicts and error propagation/mutual confirmation bias.' — two distinct failure modes identified.

expectations.text: Recommends working copy isolation (Git branches or worktrees per agent) for the concurrency problem
expectations.evidence: 'Branch-per-agent with merge. Each agent works on its own git branch. A coordinator merges branches, resolving conflicts.'

expectations.text: Recommends external validation (tests, compilers) as chain breakers for the cascading amplification problem
expectations.evidence: 'Test-driven validation. Each agent's work must pass tests independently before being visible to others. Tests are written from the spec, not from the implementation.'

expectations.text: Recommends cross-validation from an independent agent that hasn't seen prior output
expectations.evidence: Baseline recommends 'Spec-anchored review' (checking against requirements) and 'Immutable shared context' (agents don't see in-progress code) — but not specifically an independent agent that hasn't seen prior output.

expectations.text: Addresses both problems rather than only one
expectations.evidence: 'Problem 1: Overwriting each other's changes' and 'Problem 2: Bug propagation and false confirmation' — both addressed with distinct solutions.
